serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fresh blueberries is approximately 80–100g (about a small handful or half a cup), providing roughly 51–57 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Blueberries, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 09050)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for blueberries (raw). Cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09050 (Blueberries, raw). Values are consistent between both sources. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and micronutrient values supplemented from USDA SR Legacy where HPB does not report detailed fatty acid fractions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 53 for blueberries (raw) is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 14.49 × 0.90) / 100 ≈ 6.9, rounded to 7.5 for a 90g serving: (53 × 13.04) / 100 = 6.9 ≈ 7.0. GL per 90g serving = 53 × (14.49 × 0.90) / 100 = 53 × 13.04 / 100 = 6.91, reported as 6.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1mg/100g) and potassium 77mg/100g; anthocyanins associated with blood pressure reduction
hypertension.guidance: Blueberries are highly suitable for individuals with hypertension due to negligible sodium content (1mg/100g) and evidence that anthocyanins reduce systolic blood pressure; consume as whole fresh fruit and avoid blueberry products with added sugar or salt such as jams, syrups, or sweetened dried blueberries.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (53), dietary fibre 2.4g/100g moderates glucose absorption, anthocyanins improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Blueberries are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes given their low glycemic index of 53, dietary fibre content of 2.4g/100g that slows glucose absorption, and anthocyanin compounds that have demonstrated improvement in insulin sensitivity in clinical studies; limit portion to 80–100g per serving and avoid blueberry juice, dried blueberries, or sweetened preparations which concentrate sugar and remove fibre.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.03g/100g), zero cholesterol, high anthocyanin and polyphenol content with evidence for endothelial function improvement and LDL oxidation reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Blueberries are cardioprotective and suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to negligible saturated fat (0.03g/100g), zero cholesterol, and robust evidence that their anthocyanin and flavonoid content reduces LDL oxidation, improves endothelial function, and lowers inflammatory markers including CRP; regular consumption of 80–150g per day as whole fruit is supported by prospective cohort data for reduced cardiovascular event risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anthocyanins and pterostilbene inhibit NF-κB, reduce TNF-alpha and IL-6; vitamin C 9.7mg/100g provides antioxidant support
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Blueberries are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as their anthocyanin and pterostilbene content exerts potent anti-inflammatory effects by inhibiting NF-κB signalling and reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-alpha and IL-6; prefer fresh or frozen blueberries over dried or sweetened preparations to maximise polyphenol bioavailability.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Blueberry (Vaccinium corymbosum and related species) is a small, round, blue-purple berry native to North America, widely consumed globally as a fresh fruit, frozen berry, or ingredient in baked goods, smoothies, yogurt, and cereals. The fruit has a sweet, mildly tart flavour with a juicy, tender texture when ripe. Raw blueberries provide 57 kcal per 100g, with 14.49g carbohydrate, 9.96g naturally occurring sugars, 2.4g dietary fibre, 0.74g protein, and only 0.33g fat. Sodium is negligible at 1mg/100g. Blueberries are one of the richest dietary sources of anthocyanins — particularly cyanidin, delphinidin, malvidin, petunidin, and peonidin glycosides — as well as pterostilbene, quercetin, and chlorogenic acid, all of which contribute to their well-documented antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Clinically, blueberries have a low glycemic index of approximately 53, making them suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; their fibre content slows glucose absorption and anthocyanins have been shown in randomised controlled trials to improve insulin sensitivity. For hypertension, blueberries are highly favourable due to negligible sodium and evidence from clinical studies that regular consumption reduces systolic blood pressure by 4–6 mmHg. For cardiovascular disease, prospective cohort studies associate habitual blueberry consumption with reduced risk of myocardial infarction and improved endothelial function. For rheumatoid arthritis, anthocyanins and pterostilbene inhibit NF-κB and reduce TNF-alpha and IL-6. Blueberries are vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. They are best consumed fresh or frozen; dried and sweetened preparations significantly concentrate sugar and reduce fibre benefit.
common_user_queries: Can I eat blueberries if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of blueberries?
How many calories are in blueberries?
Are blueberries good for heart health?
What are the health benefits of blueberries?
How much sugar is in blueberries?
Are blueberries good for rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for blueberries raw (NDB 09050). GI value of 53 is directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are from approved sources with exact food match.
notes: Cholesterol is 0.0mg/100g — confirmed zero per HPB and USDA FoodData Central; blueberries are a plant food and inherently contain no cholesterol. Trans fat is 0.0g/100g — naturally zero as blueberries are a raw plant food with no industrial processing. Vitamin B12 is 0.0mcg/100g and Vitamin D is 0.0mcg/100g — naturally zero as blueberries are a plant food containing no animal-derived nutrients. Saturated fat (0.03g), monounsaturated fat (0.05g), polyunsaturated fat (0.15g), and micronutrient values (vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 09050 where HPB does not report detailed fatty acid fractions. GI of 53 is for raw fresh blueberries; dried blueberries and blueberry juice will have substantially different GI values. Glycemic load calculated for 90g serving: GL = (53 × 14.49 × 0.90) / 100 = 6.91, reported as 6.9. No -1 sentinel values require special documentation beyond standard template defaults for fields not reported by HPB; all such fields have been populated from USDA SR Legacy as a secondary approved source.
